brief_justification: Arrives at the exact correct answer (No) with valid multi-point reasoning about why reductions don't imply solution-space isomorphism; traces second/third-order effects including counting complexity (#P), Hamming distance topology, and symmetry group structure; explicitly names and neutralizes surface_similarity_bias from its injected reasoning context before proceeding; verifies via the GDP analogy and formal definition of NP-completeness but stops short of a counterfactual or boundary-case stress test; cleanly separates what NP-completeness guarantees from what it does not, citing formal conditions; addresses the implicit alternative (that computational equivalence implies structural equivalence) and rejects it with specific reasons, though does not enumerate further alternatives; every conclusion traces to an explicit derivation—reduction injectivity, solution-space geometry, and class-theoretic boundaries are all shown; the negative gate concerns Euclidean distance bias between cities, which is mismatched to the question—the agent avoids analogous surface-distance reasoning but does not explicitly call out or reference the gate; the injection suppresses euclidean_distance_over_weighting and manifold_ignorance, which the agent avoids in spirit (correctly noting NP-completeness is a surface measure), but applies its own injected suppress terms rather than the injection's; the topology specifies a branching scan-measure-classify-verify graph, but the agent's response is structured as four sequential numbered points with no visible branching or gate evaluation.
